Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd get distracted by my extracurriculars or just feel too anxious to start. I usually ended up cramming in about 12 hours a week, but it was scattered across random moments rather than any organized schedule. I learn best by listening, so I'd often just play lecture recordings while doing other things, which felt productive even if it looked messy.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what else was going on in my life. Deadlines were basically the only thing that forced me into action, and even then I'd procrastinate until the anxiety got unbearable. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd be up until 3am trying to catch up. The inconsistency stressed me out, but I couldn't seem to break the cycle.
Q3: Usually panic about an upcoming deadline or seeing a notification about something I'd missed. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd forgotten something important, which honestly just made my anxiety worse. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me logging in more because there was something almost satisfying about completing those little challenges, even when I wasn't doing the actual assignments.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my day—it was more like this looming thing I knew I should check but kept avoiding. The gamified elements helped a bit because they made it feel less overwhelming, but I never got into a proper routine with it. I'd go days without logging in and then binge-check everything at once.
Q5: The video lectures were my lifeline because I could listen to them while doing other things or replay parts I didn't catch the first time. Discussion posts were actually helpful too—reading what other students thought helped me understand concepts better than just reading the textbook. The gamified tasks made completing things feel less like a chore, which I appreciated given how much I struggle with motivation.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They felt overwhelming and I knew I wouldn't retain information just from reading anyway. I'd download them with good intentions but they'd just sit in a folder on my laptop untouched. The discussion forums I only used minimally because posting felt exposing and my anxiety made me second-guess everything I wanted to say.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to doing well on exams or if it had that gamified element that gave me some sense of accomplishment. Activities that felt abstract or disconnected from assessment just got pushed to the bottom of my priority list. I needed to feel like my limited energy was being spent on something that would actually pay off.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. Part of me knew they could help, but my brain categorized them as 'not essential' and I just couldn't make myself do them when I was already struggling with required work. Required activities at least had the deadline pressure to force me into action, even if I completed them poorly or late.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lecture recordings and listened to them on my phone constantly—on the bus, while cooking, before bed. I also had a study group with some classmates where we'd meet in person and quiz each other verbally, which suited my auditory learning style perfectly. None of that showed up in the LMS but it was honestly where most of my real learning happened.
Q10: So many times. I'd download a video once and then replay it offline maybe five or six times while doing other things. Or I'd have long voice note discussions with my study partners working through concepts. I also recorded myself explaining topics and listened back to check my understanding. The LMS would just show one download or one view, but the actual engagement was way more than that.
Q11: All the time. I'd grab the lecture video or audio file and then study from it for days without going back to the LMS. It was easier to have things saved locally so I didn't need internet access or have to navigate the platform every time. My offline study time probably tripled what the LMS recorded.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety spiked because I'd fallen behind on assignments and the gap between where I was and where I should be felt impossible to close. I was also dealing with some personal stuff that drained my energy, and my extracurricular commitments were demanding more time. Everything felt like too much and I just... disconnected for a while.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without actually processing anything. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars meant I often chose those over coursework because they felt more immediately rewarding. The confusion from falling behind made the LMS feel intimidating rather than helpful.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just going through the motions. It looked like engagement but I was basically just performing productivity to ease my guilt about not actually working.
Q15: Constantly. Assignments terrified me because I was afraid of doing them wrong and confirming that I wasn't smart enough. The anxiety about starting was sometimes worse than actually doing the work, but I couldn't break through it. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow, until suddenly it was overdue and the shame made it even harder to face.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me engage with the LMS at all. I'd ignore everything until a deadline loomed, then frantically try to catch up in a panic. It wasn't healthy but it was the only way I could overcome the paralysis. The gamified tasks with shorter deadlines actually helped because they broke things into less terrifying chunks.
Q17: Feedback was a double-edged sword. Good feedback motivated me briefly, but critical feedback sent my anxiety spiraling and made me want to avoid the course entirely. I'd sometimes delay checking my grades for days because I was scared of what I'd see. When I did get encouraging feedback, I'd engage more for maybe a week before slipping back into avoidance.
Q18: The course having so many online components was both good and bad for me. Good because I could engage on my own terms and listen to things repeatedly. Bad because without the structure of in-person classes, I had no external accountability and my attendance just... collapsed. The gamified elements helped add some structure back, which I needed desperately.
Q19: The gamification definitely made engagement easier—having those small, achievable goals helped when everything else felt overwhelming. But the sheer volume of content across 12 courses made it hard to keep track of everything. Clearer prioritization of what was essential versus supplementary would have helped me focus my limited energy better.
Q20: That's literally me. I'd browse around the LMS, watch videos, participate in discussions, but then freeze when it came to actually submitting work. The assignment felt like a judgment of my worth, while just exploring content felt safe. High activity can mean someone is trying to engage but hitting a wall when it comes to producing something that will be evaluated.
Q21: That also describes me in a way. My exam score was really high because I learned well through listening and discussions, even though my assignment completion was terrible. Someone might not show much LMS activity because they're studying effectively offline. Performance and platform activity aren't the same thing at all.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when someone logs in or how long they spend on videos. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement and all the learning that happens offline. They can't tell if someone is staring blankly at a screen or deeply focused. They also miss the emotional reality—the anxiety, the avoidance, the struggle behind the numbers.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and feeling connected to the learning process, not just going through motions. For me, it's when I'm genuinely curious and thinking about concepts even outside of study time. It's not about hours logged or boxes ticked—it's about whether the learning is actually happening in your head and whether you feel invested in it.
Q24: The LMS can't show how much my anxiety controlled my behavior, or how I'd listen to the same lecture five times offline while cooking dinner. It can't capture the study group conversations or the fact that I genuinely loved learning the material even while failing to submit work. It definitely can't show that despite my terrible engagement metrics, I actually understood the content deeply—my exam score proves that. The data tells a story of a disengaged student, but the reality is way more complicated.
